title: Floer Homology with DG Coefficients. Applications to Cotangent Bundles
speaker: Alexandru Oancea
abstract: Given a path-connected topological space X, a differential graded (DG) local system (or derived local system) is a module over the DGA of chains on the based loop space of X. I will explain how to define in the symplectically aspherical case Hamiltonian Floer homology with coefficients in a DG local system, how this homology fits into a filtered homological toolbox, and will present a number of dynamical applications to cotangent bundles. This is joint work with Jean-François Barraud, Mihai Damian and Vincent Humilière. The construction of Floer homology with enriched coefficients was originally discovered by Barraud-Cornea, and it was revisited over the years in different settings by Abouzaid, Charette, Zhou, and Rezchikov.

proof_stages.label: Symplectic-homology detection criterion
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates closed-characteristic existence by comparing classical homology with symplectic homology and formulates the criterion that a classical class killed in symplectic homology but nonzero in the domain forces almost existence of contractible closed characteristics.

proof_stages.label: Cotangent obstruction and main theorem statements
proof_stages.summary: For cotangent bundles, constant coefficients give symplectic homology of the disk bundle as homology of the free loop space, so the constant-loop part injects; the announced theorems use DG coefficients to restore noninjectivity under non-K(pi,1) or non-rational-K(pi,1) hypotheses.

proof_stages.label: DG local systems from Moore loop algebras
proof_stages.summary: A differential graded local system on a based space is defined as a right module over cubical chains on the Moore based-loop DGA, and fibrations supply such modules through transitive lifting functions.

proof_stages.label: Barraud-Cornea twisting cocycle and Morse complex
proof_stages.summary: Brown's twisted tensor product is recast in Morse theory by choosing coherent chains on compactified moduli spaces, evaluating them to based-loop chains, and using the resulting universal twisting cocycle to define Morse homology with DG coefficients, which recovers homology of total spaces of fibrations.

proof_stages.label: Floer-theoretic DG coefficients and computations
proof_stages.summary: The same twisting construction is applied to Hamiltonian Floer moduli spaces over the free-loop space, yielding Floer and symplectic homologies with DG coefficients together with DG PSS and Viterbo isomorphisms.

proof_stages.label: Universal path-loop coefficient system
proof_stages.summary: For the universal path-loop fibration over the contractible-loop component, the chosen local system makes cotangent-bundle symplectic homology equal to the homology of a point, while the constant-loop part becomes H_*(Omega^2 Q), which is larger when Q is not K(pi,1).

proof_stages.label: Shriek-map injectivity and almost-existence conclusion
proof_stages.summary: Degree-one shriek-map identities with DG coefficients make the map to the domain injective, so the nonzero class found in the constant-loop part satisfies the detection criterion and gives contractible almost existence, with the rational variant governed by torsion.

examples.name: Gromov's Weinstein theorem in R^{2n} via symplectic homology
examples.note: The Euclidean contact-type hypersurface case is worked through to reveal the killed-class mechanism behind the later almost-existence criterion.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explains the symplectic-homology proof in R^{2n}, using the ball’s vanishing symplectic homology and a nonzero homology class killed by a periodic orbit.

examples.name: Unit cotangent bundle and closed geodesics
examples.note: The boundary of a unit disk cotangent bundle is used to show why constant coefficients cannot force contractible characteristics in cotangent bundles, motivating the move to DG coefficients.
examples.engagement_reason: The unit cotangent bundle is used to analyze why the R^{2n} argument fails, identifying boundary orbits with closed geodesics and symplectic homology with free-loop-space homology.

passes.reason: The unit cotangent bundle is an ordinary contact-type hypersurface, used because its characteristics are geodesics and some bases have no contractible closed geodesics.
The unit cotangent bundle is a standard contact-type hypersurface, invoked to show that cotangent bundles need not have contractible closed characteristics.

A unit cotangent boundary is structurally ordinary, but it is used to show that cotangent bundles need not have contractible closed characteristics because these would be contractible closed geodesics.

The unit cotangent bundle/geodesic correspondence is invoked to show the R^{2n} contractible-orbit argument cannot extend directly to cotangent bundles.
Unit cotangent bundles/geodesic flows are used to show the contractible closed-characteristic conclusion can fail, while the object is otherwise structurally ordinary.

The unit cotangent bundle is invoked to show the naive R^{2n} scheme cannot force contractible closed characteristics, since its Reeb orbits are closed geodesics and some bases have no contractible ones.

examples.name: Embedded exact Lagrangian submanifold
examples.note: An exact closed Lagrangian in T*Q illustrates the degree-one hypothesis because its projection to Q is a homotopy equivalence.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker checks the theorem’s hypotheses for an embedded exact Lagrangian by using that its projection is a homotopy equivalence of degree one, then applies the result.

examples.name: Hopf fibration
examples.note: The Hopf fibration S^1 -> S^3 -> S^2 makes the module action of chains on based loops concrete, with a one-chain acting by the fundamental class of the fiber and recording Euler class one.
examples.engagement_reason: The Hopf fibration is explicitly worked through via loop holonomy, showing a one-chain in the based loop space acts on a point to give the fiber’s fundamental class and Euler class one.

passes.reason: The Hopf fibration is the homogeneous circle bundle over S^2 with minimal nonzero Euler class, used to illustrate the DG local-system action.

examples.name: Hyperbolic surface and flat torus geodesic flows in the K(pi,1) case
examples.note: The Q&A uses genus-two hyperbolic surfaces and the flat torus to clarify that K(pi,1) cotangent bundles should not be expected to have contractible geodesic orbits, only possibly noncontractible ones.
examples.engagement_reason: Hyperbolic surface and flat torus geodesic flows are cited in the K(pi,1) discussion as having no contractible geodesics, without further calculation or construction.
